title: Trusted Device
description: The canonical shape of a TrustedDevice record — docs/44-PHASE-2B-TECHNICAL-PLAN.md §5.1/§5.3's design, batch PXP-8 (docs/44 §22), governed by docs/47-PHASE-2B-IMPLEMENTATION-RULES.md and ADR-015. Patient-owned (a hard boundary distinct from every other PXP-1..7 entity, all of which are doctor/staff-owned): every write is the session-authenticated patient's own action, never a doctor/staff action, since a Trusted Device is a credential for the patient's own device, not clinical content. device_token_hash is the SHA-256 hash of a high-entropy, server-generated raw token (never the raw token itself, never stored) — the same entropy class and hashing pattern shared/schemas/login-token.schema.json's token_hash already established (ADR-015 §Decision 1: 'the same entropy class as LoginToken, so it safely reuses LoginToken's already-proven plain-SHA-256 hashing pattern'). The raw token rotates on every successful presentation (apps-script/TrustedDevice.gs's foundationConsumeTrustedDevice_()) — device_token_hash's value at any moment reflects only the current, not-yet-used raw token. See trusted-device.md for the full lifecycle, the Long-Lived Session relationship, and the disclosed revocation-latency limitation.

device_id.description: This device's stable, server-generated identity (UUID) — never derived from the raw device token, so it is safe to expose to the patient's own 'manage my devices' view and to accept back as a revoke target, without ever revealing device_token_hash itself.

patient_id.description: The Patient Identity this device belongs to (docs/33 §1.2, ADR-002). Session-derived at every write and read — a patient can only ever create, list, or revoke their own devices.

device_token_hash.description: Hex-encoded SHA-256 digest of the current raw device token (never the raw token itself — mirrors login-token.schema.json's token_hash exactly, reusing the same foundationHashLoginToken_() algorithm). Rotates on every successful foundationConsumeTrustedDevice_() call. The patient-facing get_trusted_devices route deliberately omits this field from its response (an implementation detail, not a schema violation — mirrors download_report never returning a raw Drive URL).

device_label.description: Optional, patient-supplied free text (e.g. 'My iPhone', 'Work laptop') for the 'manage my devices' view docs/44 §5.3 names as in-scope for this batch ('revocability without visibility is not meaningfully usable'). Empty-string sentinel when not supplied.

created_at.description: Server-set at this device's creation (foundationNowIso_()). Never patient-supplied directly.

last_used_at.description: Server-set on every successful foundationConsumeTrustedDevice_() call. Empty-string sentinel until the device's first use after creation.

expires_at.description: A sliding expiry — extended to now + the device TTL on every successful use, mirroring what makes 'persistent login' actually persistent for a returning patient (deliberately distinct from the Session mechanism's own non-renewing, fixed-TTL design, ADR-010, which this batch leaves entirely unchanged). A dormant device whose expires_at passes can no longer be exchanged for a session — the patient falls back to Magic Link, the permanent root of trust.

revoked_at.description: Empty-string sentinel when not revoked, mirroring doctor-assigned-condition.schema.json's resolved_at convention. Set once, server-side, on a successful revoke_trusted_device call — a one-way transition, never reverted.

revoked_by.description: The session-derived patient_id who revoked this device (always the device's own owning patient in this batch — there is no doctor/staff revocation path, since a device is the patient's own, not clinical content). Empty-string sentinel until revoked.
